title: Comedian Matt Buechele's New Podcast Promises Laughs, Absurdity, and Sharp Takes on Modern Life
summary: Imagine a podcast that might suddenly burst into song, veer into absurd tangents, but always, always deliver laughs. That's the promise of "That Sounds Like a Lot," the new weekly video podcast from comedian, writer, and composer Matt Buechele. Premiering Friday, May 1, the show is a collaboration between Vox Media and creator studio AND Media, and it's already generating buzz. Buechele, who has amassed nearly 1 million followers and over 200 million views across platforms for his satirical takes on culture, politics, masculinity, and the internet, is no stranger to the spotlight. He previously wrote for "The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon" and releases music under the artist name Booshell. Now, he's stepping into the podcasting arena with a format that blends his signature walk-and-talk monologues with in-depth, humorous interviews. Each episode will kick off with Buechele unpacking the stories shaping our world—from the crucial to the mundane—before segueing into a conversation with a guest about what truly deserves attention, what's wildly overhyped, and what we're collectively getting wrong. The first guest is comedian Zach Zimmerman, with future episodes featuring Celine Song, Jay Jurden, Alexandria Love, and Chloé Cooper Jones. "I'm very excited to be partnering with Vox and AND Media on 'That Sounds Like a Lot,'" Buechele said in a statement. Lillian Xu, VP of podcasts at Vox Media, praised his "uncanny ability to notice and precisely describe the things that shape our lives every day." AND Media CEO Christian Tom added that the series is about giving Buechele's established voice more room to grow through longer-form storytelling and new creative formats. The show is executive produced by a team with credits including "SmartLess" and "Armchair Expert." Vox Media's podcast slate already includes hits like "Pivot" with Kara Swisher and Scott Galloway, and "A Touch More" with Sue Bird and Megan Rapinoe. With "That Sounds Like a Lot," Buechele joins a roster of influential voices, but his unique blend of humor and insight promises to carve out its own niche. New episodes drop every Friday on YouTube, Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and other major platforms. In a media landscape crowded with content, Buechele's show aims to cut through the noise by being, well, a lot—in the best possible way.
why_it_matters: As young people navigate an endless stream of online content, shows like this demonstrate how humor and thoughtful interviews can help people make sense of the world, and they also show how creators can evolve from short viral clips to more meaningful, long-form storytelling that influences culture.

keywords.explanation: Using humor to make fun of or criticize something, often to show what is wrong with it.
Long speeches by one person, sometimes delivered directly to the audience.
Smoothly moving from one topic or section to another.
Given much more attention or praise than something really deserves.
A small, specialized part of a larger market or audience.
Strange and mysterious in a way that surprises you, almost as if it seems supernaturally accurate.

background_read: Podcasts have exploded in popularity over the last decade, with millions of people tuning in weekly to hear conversations, stories, and comedy. Vox Media (a digital media company founded in 2011, known for explanatory journalism and podcasts like 'Today, Explained') has built a strong lineup of influential shows. Matt Buechele originally gained fame through short satirical videos on platforms like TikTok and Instagram, where he commented on everything from politics to masculinity. He also wrote for 'The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon' (a flagship NBC late-night talk show that launched in 2014), which gave him experience in crafting humor for a broad audience. His shift into podcasting reflects a broader trend of digital creators moving into longer, more in-depth formats to connect with audiences in new ways.

└─ specific: Tone of absurdity and laughter

NICHE: Conclusion — show aims to cut through noise by being unique and energetic

perspectives.description: The podcast offers a fresh, funny take on current events and culture, combining Buechele’s sharp comedic style with longer, deeper conversations than his short online videos. Collaborating with Vox Media and experienced producers may give him the resources to reach a wider audience and produce high-quality content.

The podcast market is extremely crowded, and a celebrity-driven show might struggle to stand out despite its host’s talent. Some critics might argue that using humor to discuss complex social and political issues can sometimes oversimplify or trivialize them, even if unintentionally.

Buechele’s move reflects a broader shift in media, where digital-first creators are being recruited by established networks to produce longer content. Vox Media’s strategy of expanding its podcast roster with creator-partnered shows aims to capture younger audiences who might not listen to traditional talk formats.

If the show succeeds, it could pave the way for more collaborations between major media companies and internet-native comedians, changing how humor is packaged. It might also challenge news and analysis podcasts by blending entertainment with genuine insight, raising the bar for what a modern talk show can be.
